div.h1: Undo Order: Why Global LSN Matters
div.p: When multiple transactions modify the same page, undo MUST proceed in reverse global LSN order. This diagram demonstrates how improper ordering leads to database corruption.

g.text: ❌ FAILURE PATH: Undo T1 (Older) Before T2 (Newer)
✓ SUCCESS PATH: Reverse Global LSN Order (LSN 200 -> 100)

div.p: The ARIES recovery algorithm mandates that UNDO proceeds in descending LSN order globally across the log.

ol.li: Maintain a Max-Heap containing the LastLSN of all active transactions.
Pop the largest LSN from the heap.
Undo that log record's specific operation.
Push that record's PrevLSN back into the heap.
Continue until the heap is empty.

p.em: This ensures that "Newer" changes are peeled off before "Older" changes, maintaining the physical integrity of before-images.

div.p: Final Value: B Expected Value: A (Original state)
Root Cause: T1's undo overwrote the state that T2's before-image relied upon. T2 restored B over A, but B was itself a temporary state that should have been undone.
Final Value: A Match: Initial A
Why this works: Undoing LSN 200 first returns the page to the EXACT physical state it was in at LSN 100, making T1's before-image valid.
Input: Page Data = C Log Action: Restore A Resulting Page: A
Input: Page Data = A Log Action: Restore B (Before-image) Resulting Page: B
Input: Page Data = C Log Action: Restore B
Input: Page Data = B Log Action: Restore A
